articles.author: Aamer Madhani
articles.description: President Donald Trump started the fourth week of his war against Iran by offering the world some guarded optimism that the U.S. could soon be winding down its military operations. It was a claim that buoyed markets on Monday. But Iranian officials dismissed it as a ploy to buy time. Trump said he was putting off for five days his threat to bomb Iran’s power plants unless Tehran quickly opened the Strait of Hormuz to oil tankers. He said his envoys have had “very good” talks with unnamed Iranian officials. But Tehran denied that. If Trump were to end the war now, he would be walking away at a moment when he still hasn’t fully achieved his stated objectives.
articles.title: Trump says there are talks with Iran. Tehran says he's buying time

articles.author: Lorian Belanger
articles.description: Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said that maritime traffic isn’t passing through the Strait of Hormuz because insurance companies are concerned about the U.S.-initiated war, not because of Iran’s actions.
articles.title: Live updates: US Central Command leader says Iran campaign is 'ahead or on plan'

articles.author: Jon Gambrell, David Rising, Samy Magdy
articles.description: President Donald Trump has extended his deadline for Iran to reopen the crucial Strait of Hormuz to international shipping. He said Monday that the U.S. would hold off on threatened strikes against Iranian power plants for five days. In his announcement, Trump also held out the possibility of a resolution to the war. Iranian officials denied there were negotiations. Trump’s turnaround appeared to offer something of a reprieve after rhetoric reached a fever pitch when the U.S. and Iran traded threats with potentially catastrophic repercussions for civilians across the region.
articles.title: Trump extends his deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz

articles.description: Authorities in New York say an Air Canada flight collided with a Port Authority vehicle after landing at LaGuardia Airport. The New York Police Department has confirmed the collision but could not immediately offer additional information. A spokesperson for the New York City Fire Department said firefighters responded to reports of a plane that crashed into a vehicle on the runway at 11:38 p.m. Additional information was not immediately available. Multiple videos taken by bystanders and posted on social media show the jet with severe damage to the front of the aircraft.
articles.title: Air Canada flight and Port Authority vehicle collide at LaGuardia

articles.author: Jennifer Peltz, Ed White
articles.description: It's being called a miracle. A flight attendant who was still strapped in her seat has survived after being thrown from an Air Canada plane. The plane collided with a fire truck at New York’s LaGuardia Airport, and the cockpit was destroyed. Two people were killed. Solange Tremblay was a flight attendant on the plane. Her daughter says Tremblay has a broken leg and needs surgery but is otherwise OK. Aviation safety expert Jeff Guzzetti says the flight attendant would have been strapped in a seat with a four-point restraint during landing.
articles.title: Flight attendant survives being thrown from Air Canada plane

articles.author: Philip Marcelo
articles.description: A jet landing at New York City’s LaGuardia Airport has collided with a fire truck on the runway, killing the pilot and co-pilot and injuring several others. The crash occurred late Sunday night when an Air Canada regional jet struck an airport fire truck. Officials say the emergency vehicle had been traveling across the runway to respond to a separate incident aboard another plane. Photos and videos from the crash’s aftermath show the jet’s nose crushed and the fire truck on its side. Besides the two deaths, about 40 passengers and crew members were hospitalized.
articles.title: What to know about the deadly crash at NYC's LaGuardia airport

articles.author: Jonathan J. Cooper
articles.description: The U.S. Defense Department says it will issue new press credentials but remove media offices from the Pentagon. The announcement comes after a judge sided with The New York Times in a lawsuit regarding limits on reporters’ access to the building. Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell says the agency disagrees with the ruling and will appeal. U.S. District Judge Paul Friedman in Washington, D.C., last week sided with the newspaper and ruled that the Pentagon’s new policy illegally restricts the press credentials of reporters who walked out of the building rather than agree to the new rules. Parnell says reporters will be able to work from an “annex” outside the building, which “will be available when ready.”
articles.title: Pentagon to issue new press credentials but remove media offices

articles.author: Andrew Dalton
articles.description: A California civil jury has found Bill Cosby liable for drugging and sexually assaulting a woman in 1972. On Monday, jurors in Santa Monica awarded Donna Motsinger $19.25 million after a nearly two-week trial. Motsinger said in a lawsuit filed in 2023 that Cosby invited her to a show, gave her wine and pills. She says she later woke up at home partly undressed and concluded that Cosby had drugged and raped her. His lawyers called the claims speculation and say they plan to appeal. The verdict came nearly five years after Cosby was freed from prison in a criminal case with similar allegations.
articles.title: Bill Cosby found liable for 1972 sexual assault by California jury

articles.author: Dave Collins
articles.description: A federal judge has appointed veteran prosecutor Robert Frazer as U.S. attorney for New Jersey, ending a dispute between the judiciary and Trump administration. Frazer was named to the position on Monday as part of an agreement between judges and the Justice Department. President Donald Trump's first pick for the job, his former personal attorney Alina Habba, was disqualified by a judge last year because she had stayed too long without Senate confirmation. Then last month, the same judge disqualified three Justice Department officials who were sharing authority over the office, saying they were appointed in an illegal power grab by the administration.
articles.title: New US attorney for New Jersey named after others were disqualified

articles.author: Mark Sherman
articles.description: The Supreme Court’s conservative majority sounds skeptical of state laws that allow the counting of late-arriving mail ballots, a target of President Donald Trump. The court heard arguments Monday in a case from Mississippi that also could affect voters in 13 other states and the District of Columbia, which have grace periods for ballots cast by mail. An additional 15 states that have more forgiving deadlines for ballots from military and overseas voters also could be impacted. A ruling is expected by late June, early enough to govern the counting of ballots in the 2026 midterm congressional elections. The court challenge is part of Trump’s broader attack on most mail balloting.
articles.title: Supreme Court's conservative majority questions state laws on late mail ballots
